learn.title: Perfect Squares & Cubes
learn.intro: Recognizing squares and cubes on sight — without a calculator — saves huge amounts of time across Number System, unit-digit, and even Data Interpretation questions.

sections.explanation: A perfect square is the result of multiplying an integer by itself (n×n=n²). Recognizing them quickly helps with simplification, unit-digit checks, and factor-count problems.
sections.realLifeExample: Tiling a square room with side length 12 ft using 1 sq ft tiles needs exactly 12²=144 tiles — perfect squares appear naturally in area calculations.
sections.memoryTrick: Perfect squares only end in 0, 1, 4, 5, 6, or 9 — never in 2, 3, 7, or 8.
sections.shortcutTrick: To quickly check if a number could be a perfect square, first check its last digit against the allowed set; if it fails, it's definitely not a perfect square — this eliminates many options instantly.
commonMistakes: Assuming a number ending in an allowed digit (0,1,4,5,6,9) IS automatically a perfect square — the digit test only rules non-squares out, it never confirms one.
Forgetting to double-check with an actual square root estimate after the last-digit filter passes.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint often gives large numbers and asks 'is this a perfect square' — combine the last-digit filter with an approximate square root estimate to decide fast.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT tests memorized squares up to 25² or 30² — memorize this range to skip calculation entirely during the test.

sections.explanation: A perfect cube is the result of multiplying an integer by itself three times (n×n×n=n³). Cubes grow much faster than squares, so fewer of them appear in a given range.
sections.realLifeExample: Packing a cube-shaped storage box with side 5 units using 1-unit cubes needs exactly 5³=125 small cubes — perfect cubes appear naturally in volume calculations.
sections.memoryTrick: Perfect cubes keep the same last digit as their root for most digits, except 2↔8 and 3↔7, which swap.
sections.shortcutTrick: To estimate a cube root: group the number in sets of 3 digits from the right to gauge the root's digit count, then match the last digit using the swap rule (2↔8, 3↔7, others unchanged) to pin down the last digit of the root.
formulaCard: Last-digit cube pairs: 2³ ends in 8, 8³ ends in 2; 3³ ends in 7, 7³ ends in 3 — all other digits match their root's last digit
commonMistakes: Forgetting the 2↔8 and 3↔7 swap when estimating a cube root from the last digit.
Confusing cube root estimation with square root estimation — the digit-grouping and swap rules are different.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint sometimes asks for the cube root of numbers up to 6 digits — memorizing cubes up to 15³=3375 covers nearly all such questions directly.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT occasionally tests 'is this number a perfect cube' — check the last-digit swap rule first as a fast filter before attempting to estimate the actual root.

solvedExamples.question: Is 1024 a perfect square? If so, find its square root.
solvedExamples.solution:
1024 ends in 4, an allowed last digit for perfect squares.
32×32 = 1024, confirming it.

solvedExamples.question: Find the smallest perfect square greater than 200.
solvedExamples.solution:
14² = 196 (less than 200). 15² = 225 (greater than 200).
So the smallest perfect square greater than 200 is 225.

solvedExamples.question: Find the cube root of 2744.
solvedExamples.solution:
2744 lies between 2197 (13³) and 3375 (14³).
Its last digit is 4, which matches a root ending in 4 (no swap needed).
14×14×14 = 2744, confirming the cube root is 14.

solvedExamples.question: Is 500 a perfect cube?
solvedExamples.solution: 7³ = 343 and 8³ = 512. 500 falls strictly between them, so it isn't equal to either — not a perfect cube.

solvedExamples.question: Find a number between 500 and 800 that is both a perfect square and a perfect cube.
solvedExamples.solution:
A number that is both a square and a cube must be a perfect 6th power.
1⁶=1, 2⁶=64, 3⁶=729 — and 729 falls between 500 and 800.

practiceExamples.question: Is 625 a perfect square? Find its root if so.
practiceExamples.hint: Check the last digit (5 is allowed), then test 25².

practiceExamples.question: Find the largest perfect square less than 300.
Find the smallest number greater than 1 that is both a perfect square and a perfect cube.

easy.question: Which of these numbers is NOT a perfect square?
easy.explanation: 81=9², 100=10², 121=11², but 72 falls between 8²=64 and 9²=81, so it isn't a perfect square.
whyWrong: 81=9², so it is a perfect square.
100=10², so it is a perfect square.
121=11², so it is a perfect square.
easy.shortcut: Memorizing squares up to 15² makes spotting non-squares instant.

easy.question: What is the square root of 169?
easy.shortcut: Memorize squares up to 20² to answer these instantly.

easy.question: What is the cube of 4?
whyWrong: 48 doesn't equal 4×4×4.
56 doesn't equal 4×4×4 either.
72 doesn't equal 4×4×4 either.
easy.shortcut: Memorize cubes up to 10³ for instant recall.

easy.question: Which of these numbers is a perfect cube?
whyWrong: 200 isn't a perfect cube (5³=125, 6³=216).
225 is a perfect square (15²) but not a perfect cube.
250 isn't a perfect cube either.
easy.shortcut: Memorize cubes up to 10³=1000 to recognize them quickly.

easy.question: A perfect square can never end in which of these digits?
easy.explanation: Perfect squares only end in 0, 1, 4, 5, 6, 9 — 3 is never a valid last digit for a perfect square.
whyWrong: 4 is a valid last digit for perfect squares (e.g. 4, 64, 144).
9 is a valid last digit for perfect squares (e.g. 9, 49, 169).
6 is a valid last digit for perfect squares (e.g. 16, 36, 196).
easy.shortcut: Memorize the six allowed last digits for perfect squares: 0,1,4,5,6,9.

medium.question: Find the smallest perfect square greater than 150.
medium.explanation: 12²=144 (less than 150), 13²=169 (greater than 150), so 169 is the smallest perfect square greater than 150.
whyWrong: 144 is less than 150, not greater.
196=14² is greater than 150 but not the smallest such square.
160 isn't a perfect square at all.
medium.shortcut: Find the integer square root of the target number, round up, then square that.

medium.question: Find the cube root of 4096.
medium.shortcut: Group the digits in sets of 3 from the right and use the last-digit rule (6 stays 6) to estimate quickly.

medium.question: How many perfect squares lie between 50 and 150?
medium.explanation: The perfect squares between 50 and 150 are 64, 81, 100, 121, 144 — a total of 5.
whyWrong: 4 undercounts by missing one square.
6 overcounts by including a square outside the range.
3 undercounts by missing two squares.
medium.shortcut: Find ⌈√50⌉ and ⌊√150⌋, then count the integers between them inclusive.

medium.question: Is 1728 a perfect cube? If so, find its cube root.
medium.explanation: 12×12×12=1728, so yes, 1728 is a perfect cube with cube root 12.
whyWrong: 1728 is indeed a perfect cube, so 'No' is incorrect.
medium.shortcut: Memorizing cubes up to 15³ covers most aptitude test cube-root questions directly.

medium.question: What is the sum of the first 5 perfect square numbers (starting from 1²)?
whyWrong: 50 doesn't match the sum 1+4+9+16+25.
60 doesn't match it either.
45 doesn't match it either.
medium.shortcut: Use the formula for the sum of the first n squares: n(n+1)(2n+1)/6 = 5×6×11/6 = 55.

medium.question: A number when squared gives 2809. Find the number.
medium.shortcut: Estimate the root using nearby memorized squares (50²=2500), then fine-tune digit by digit.

medium.question: Which of the following is both a perfect square and ends in the digit 6?
medium.explanation: 196=14², and it ends in 6, satisfying both conditions.
whyWrong: 225=15² is a perfect square but ends in 5, not 6.
289=17² is a perfect square but ends in 9, not 6.
324=18² is a perfect square but ends in 4, not 6.
medium.shortcut: A square ends in 6 only when its root ends in 4 or 6 (since 4²=16, 6²=36) — check both cases.

medium.question: Find the smallest 4-digit perfect cube.
medium.explanation: 10³=1000 is exactly a 4-digit number and the smallest cube reaching 4 digits (9³=729 is only 3 digits).
whyWrong: 729=9³ is only a 3-digit number.
1331=11³ is a valid 4-digit cube, but not the smallest one.
900 isn't a perfect cube at all.
medium.shortcut: Test consecutive integers' cubes near the digit-count boundary to find the transition point.

medium.question: If x² = 1444, find the positive value of x.
medium.shortcut: Since 1444 ends in 4, its root ends in either 2 or 8 — test values near √1444≈38 directly.

medium.question: Find the cube root of 9261.
medium.shortcut: 9261 ends in 1, so its cube root also ends in 1 (no swap needed) — test 21 directly, close to the estimated range.

hard.question: Find the sum of the digits of 999².
whyWrong: 26 doesn't match the actual digit sum of 998001.
28 doesn't match it either.
25 doesn't match it either.

hard.question: How many numbers between 1 and 1000 are both perfect squares and perfect cubes?
hard.explanation: Numbers that are both perfect squares and perfect cubes are perfect 6th powers. Below 1000: 1⁶=1, 2⁶=64, 3⁶=729 — three such numbers (4⁶=4096 exceeds 1000).
whyWrong: 2 undercounts — it misses one of the three valid 6th powers.
4 overcounts — 4⁶=4096 exceeds 1000.
1 severely undercounts, missing two valid numbers.
hard.shortcut: 'Both square and cube' always means a 6th power — test 1⁶, 2⁶, 3⁶... until exceeding the limit.

hard.question: Find the smallest number by which 1350 must be multiplied to make it a perfect square.
hard.explanation: 1350=2¹×3³×5². To make every exponent even, multiply by 2¹×3¹=6, giving 2²×3⁴×5² — a perfect square.
whyWrong: 3 alone fixes 3's exponent but leaves 2's exponent odd.
2 alone fixes 2's exponent but leaves 3's exponent odd.
5 doesn't address either odd exponent.
hard.shortcut: Factorize fully, then multiply only by the primes with odd exponents (each needed just once more) to make every exponent even.

hard.question: Find the smallest number by which 2560 must be divided to make it a perfect cube.
hard.explanation: 2560=2⁹×5¹. The exponent of 2 (9) is already a multiple of 3, but 5's exponent (1) isn't. Dividing by 5 leaves 2⁹=512=8³, a perfect cube.
whyWrong: Dividing by 2 disturbs the already-valid exponent of 2, making it no longer a multiple of 3.
Dividing by 10 unnecessarily removes a factor of 2 as well, which wasn't needed.
Dividing by 4 also disturbs 2's valid exponent unnecessarily.
hard.shortcut: Only adjust the primes whose exponents aren't already multiples of 3 — leave the others untouched.

hard.question: If a³ is a perfect square and a is not 1, what is the smallest possible value of a?
hard.explanation: Since a³=a²×a and a² is automatically a perfect square, a³ is a perfect square exactly when a itself is a perfect square. The smallest such a greater than 1 is 4, and indeed 4³=64=8².
whyWrong: 2³=8, which is not a perfect square, so a=2 doesn't satisfy the condition.
8 is not itself a perfect square, so a=8 doesn't give a perfect square when cubed (8³=512, and 512 isn't a perfect square).
9³=729=27², so a=9 does work, but it's larger than the smallest valid value, 4.
hard.shortcut: Since a³ is a perfect square exactly when a is, just test perfect squares directly (4, 9, 16...).

hard.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 100³.
whyWrong: 3 doesn't match 10⁶'s trailing zero count.
4 doesn't match it either.
9 overcounts significantly.
hard.shortcut: Convert the base to a power of 10 first (100=10²), then multiply the exponents: (10²)³=10⁶.

hard.question: Which is the smallest perfect square that is also divisible by 12?
hard.explanation: 12=2²×3. For a perfect square divisible by 12, every prime's exponent must be even, requiring at least 2² and 3² — giving 36=6², which is indeed divisible by 12.
whyWrong: 24 is divisible by 12 but isn't a perfect square.
48 is divisible by 12 but isn't a perfect square either.
144 is a perfect square divisible by 12, but it's larger than the smallest valid answer, 36.
hard.shortcut: Round up each prime's required exponent to the next even number when building the smallest qualifying perfect square.

hard.question: The difference between the squares of two consecutive numbers is 45. Find the two numbers.
hard.explanation: The difference between consecutive squares n² and (n+1)² is always 2n+1. Setting 2n+1=45 gives n=22, so the numbers are 22 and 23.
whyWrong: 21 and 22 give a difference of 2(21)+1=43, not 45.
23 and 24 give a difference of 2(23)+1=47, not 45.
20 and 21 give a difference of 2(20)+1=41, not 45.
hard.shortcut: The gap between consecutive squares is always 2n+1 — solve directly for n instead of testing pairs.

hard.question: Find the smallest positive integer that must be added to 500 to make it a perfect square.
hard.explanation: The nearest perfect square above 500 is 23²=529 (since 22²=484 is below 500). 529−500=29.
whyWrong: 25 doesn't correspond to reaching the next perfect square above 500.
30 overshoots past the nearest perfect square.
20 doesn't reach a perfect square at all (500+20=520).
hard.shortcut: Find the integer square root of the number, round up, square that, then subtract the original number.

hard.question: A perfect cube has 4 digits and its cube root is an odd number. How many such perfect cubes exist?
hard.explanation: 4-digit cubes come from cube roots 10 through 21 (10³=1000 to 21³=9261). The odd roots in that range are 11, 13, 15, 17, 19, 21 — 6 values.
whyWrong: 5 undercounts by missing one odd root in the range.
7 overcounts by including a root outside the valid range.
4 undercounts by missing two odd roots.
hard.shortcut: First find the full range of cube roots giving a 4-digit result, then filter for odd values within that range.

quickSheet: Perfect squares only end in 0, 1, 4, 5, 6, 9 — use this as a fast elimination filter, never a confirmation.
Memorize squares up to 20-25 and cubes up to 15 for instant recall during the test.
Cube-root last digit swaps: 2↔8, 3↔7; every other digit matches its root.
'Both square and cube' always means a perfect 6th power.
To make a number a perfect square/cube, factorize it and fix only the primes whose exponents don't already fit (even for squares, multiples of 3 for cubes).
oneMinute: Squares: check last digit first, then estimate the root.
Cubes: group digits in 3s from the right, then apply the last-digit swap rule.
The gap between consecutive squares is always 2n+1.
revision.summary: Squares and cubes questions reward memorization plus a few structural rules — the six allowed last digits for squares, the swap rule for cube roots, and the 'fix only the odd exponents' trick for turning a number into a perfect square or cube. Once squares up to 25² and cubes up to 15³ are second nature, most questions in this topic become instant recall rather than calculation.
